author: @timkelly6660
text: I believe the reason that the material is not melting is not due to lack of current, but instead due to loss of magnetism. We used to heat tools in a forge and check with a magnet.  When the magnet would not stick, it was time to quench.  We now use induction and have compared the two. The temp from induction is the same as when the magnet no longer sticks.

author: @Barskor1
text: Ok so you need electricity to flow so you get work out of it why do we have to throw it away to the ground? Why not just send it to a super cap or other system? Then when that gets filled just pass the rest to ground or switch it to be the power supply and the other source to  being storage. You would lose electricity to whatever work energy was done and other inefficiencies or resistances so is it worth it or even possible?

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text: Visualization, Two buckets connected by a teeter totter sluice  with a water wheel in the middle as the work load one bucket is full (power supply) the other empty (ground). Water (electricity) flows down from full bucket spins the wheel then to the empty bucket. Formerly empty bucket now rises (Circuit switch) and then water flows back to the now empty starting bucket.

author: @HudsonKnowsEconomics
text: The target needs to be 1/2 the diameter of coil to capture more of the magnetic field especially if it is non-ferrous. A pipe will melt faster because the external surface area to volume is a lot higher so heat input can go faster than heat out. Final temp is based on heat transfer ratio (in/out), not power of the device. So insulate with crucible and tail pipe fiberglass.  Spoon did not melt partly because the surface area to volume was still not much better. Magnetic field is proportional to current not voltage like you said near the end. That's why the larger capacitors work, although by being higher current, a higher voltage on the coil does result, probably 2x to 3x the power supply voltage due to sharp mosfet cutoff.  If the mosfets blow out from this, decrease number of turns in coil which may not lower magnetic field because current change is increased with the higher frequency. The lower frequency means the skin effect goes deeper into the material (~0.3 for copper  to 2 mm for steel for 5 kHz) , not necessarily less efficient in terms of watts used, as long as the surface area of the coil divided by length is a good bit more than the ratio for the target, which is why you should use litz wiring instead of copper pipes.

replies.text:
Actually, I took that 10 minute idea and failed with it.  It is now 15.  It seemed to work, too.  It separates the refined from the unrefined.  Perhaps the 15 isn't right, but I'm sure there is a number. 
I got here from videos on building a foundry.  I entertained the idea of understanding how induction works.  My ideal video would probably have started out explaining what induction is and how it heats stuff.  I saw a video of someone taking the coil out of a microwave and rewiring the secondary coil.  I still haven't found one that speaks my language.This isn't your fault.  Thanks for the effort.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +dshreve34 No worries mate and thanks for the comment - i do actually think you are kind of right - the info should be condensed. But it is a difficult balance to strike the video has to be brief, to the point, entertaining, informative and allow replication - not an easy thing to do lol - i sometimes split my videos into a couple of minutes of 'hey look at this isn't that amazing' and longer 'well here's how you do it' - just to let peopple make the choice.

author: @johnbolliger2187
text: I had big problems getting this thing to work until I realised  that the problem was oscillations getting past the choke and affecting the gates. Simple solution: use a separate voltage supply to charge the gates. A 12V lantern battery will do nicely and then the circuit is further simplified: all you need is a resistor or two to limit the gate current

author: @undernetjack
text:
Great presentation. You do, however, assume the audience has quite a bit of electronics knowledge. Not really a problem, I suppose, because what beginner would sit through the entire video not even knowing that an inductor is just a coil of wire for instance. Perhaps just a brief statement at the beginning that a basic understanding electronics is required to grasp the lesson. Just my thoughts, not a criticism.
Hey, why not use one of Robert's customized graphene capacitors? I think that would be something to see, eh? Thank you for your efforts.

author: @MrSteltzer
text:
Do you have any idea how long I have been looking for a video/website/instructions about how the ZVS part of the system works, and explained in a way that is not over my head!! It makes so much more sense.

I still don't get how the mosfet gates end up at 0, and not just a reduced voltage/current.....I am very new to this. Might have to watch your victorian teacher impersonation again, for the 4th time LOL

I have been researching induction circuits for about 4 months, when I have some free time, as I am trying to  build a tool with very specific requirements. I need to heat brass (rifle case mouths) to 399C in 1-2 seconds, repeatedly.

I will get there, Thanks for the video!

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: MrSteltzer cool, cheers

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text:
Ashley Mills they end up at zero, because each time an inductor collapses, the back EMF drives a big potential difference between the two sides, once that is big enough, there will be essentially no voltage on the opposite gate, since it is pulled down by the diode that crosses to the other side

the crutial part is the back EMF, since that creates a force that pulls the swing further each time

replies.author: @MrSteltzer
replies.text: ahhhhh!! got it! read it a few times, and refered to your white board drawing ..again....i got it!

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: awesome!

replies.author: @MrSteltzer
replies.text: Legend! LOL sorry, I am very happy, did some annealing testing today. Getting a 3d printer to print up the rest of the parts to automate the process. you deserve a commission LOL

author: @RadiationOverdose
text: On the surface, the electronics look a lot like the zero switching transformer you see in electric cars.

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: RadiationOverdose Good point, there is likely to be some semblances, the prius for example steps up it's battery voltage using a boost converter to drive the motor. Almost all switched mode power supplies use some kind of collapsing inductive field to drive the output. The circuit shown in the video can also be used as a powerful flyback transformer by using a transformer core and having a high turn winding on the secondary.

author: @planetengineeringofficial8545
text: ive set a chalenge to myself to make an 8 x irfp260 induction heater do you recon i willl be able to melt aluminium / steel with it ?

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text: сталкер чворович i think you will almost certainly melt steel with 8 irfp260s ! VDS of 200 and a repetitive avalanche current of 46A, and I'm guessing you're looking at a config of 4 in a parallel config, wow. if you build this correctly I'd estimate you could easily get over 10KW, so you should even be able to levitate molten aluminium and steel. Only way to find out is to build it!!

replies.author: @planetengineeringofficial8545
replies.text: Wait they are 300 watts each shouldnt that be 2.4 kw? Or am i missing something ?

replies.author: @AshleyMillsTube
replies.text:
сталкер чворович the mosfets are not constantly on and power is only transferred out when the magnetic field is changing. let's say you use a voltage of 100V, with a pulse of 46A that is already 4.6kw per pulse, four blocks in parallel would be 18.4kw per pulse, if you use a higher voltage then it will be more.

But the tank currents you will be switching will be much higher, hundreds of amps, so you could say that the power transferred is much greater. I'm not really sure what other people mean when they say they have made a 10kw induction heater. Do they mean the sustained power draw or the power drawn per pulse?

replies.author: @planetengineeringofficial8545
replies.text: Ashley Mills i think they mean the sustained power draw  because i think the mesuring of pulse draw can get messy  for real power calculation
